svg.aria-label: SM_120 17-bit Control Code Semantics: bit-field decomposition, scheduler decision workflow, and producer-consumer example
svg.text: SM_120 Control Code Semantics — 17-bit Scheduling Metadata per Instruction
How the warp scheduler uses the 6 embedded fields to manage instruction issue timing and dependence tracking [S13/case_1-13]

g.text: RdBarrier: SB ID this insn waits on
blocks issue until all flagged SBs complete
Scheduler Issue Decision Flow (per cycle, per warp)
operands with bit=1 cached in RFC
→ issues immediately, SB3 tracks completion asynchronously
FFMA R3, R3, R4, R5 ; stall=4, WrBarrier=7 (fixed-lat, no SB)
IADD3 R6, R6, R7, RZ ; stall=2, WrBarrier=7 (2-cyc ALU)
CONSUMER: FFMA R11, R10, R12, R13
→ blocks until SB3==0 (LDG data in R10 is valid)
Memory subsystem completes → SB3-- → consumer unblocked → R10 valid
Reuse Flags [125:122] — Per-Operand Register File Cache (RFC) Hints
bit = 1 → cache in RFC (0-cycle on next access)
bit = 0 → no caching hint (read from RF each time)
ALU (FFMA/FADD/IMAD): functionally active → +85% ILP throughput improvement
TC (HMMA/QMMA/OMMA): true no-op — ptxas never sets, 0% effect if forced
Fixed-latency ops use stall counter (compiler-trusted); variable-latency ops use 6 HW scoreboards.
Wrong stall value = silent corruption (no trap!). stall=0 is the only safe fallback (17× penalty).
17 bits/instruction + 6 SB counters/warp replaces a full CAM-based scoreboard (saves ~10K transistor-bits/SM).
Reuse bits feed the RFC, turning repeated operand reads into 0-cycle hits — ALU pipe benefits +85%.
